blocks.text: They walked around in the midst of the flames, singing hymns to God and blessing the Lord.

1 : are Daniel's friends, first introduced in Dan 1.3–7. The MT of Daniel uses both Babylonian names and Hebrew names, but the Additions (v. 66) use only their Hebrew names, emphasizing their Jewish identity: Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah. , this addition begins after 3.23 where the friends were thrown into the furnace.
Then Azariah stood still in the fire and prayed aloud:

2 : It is not clear why is singled out to deliver the prayer except that it is a name that would have general appeal to Jewish readers since it is common in the Bible and means “the Lord has helped.” See also Tob 5–6 where the angel Raphael takes the name Azariah.
“Blessed are you, O Lord, God of our ancestors, and worthy of praise; and glorious is your name forever!
3 : is a common designation of God found especially in Deuteronomistic writings (Deut 1.11,21;4.1;6.3; Dan 2.23). , God's name becomes the focus of worship in Second Temple materials; see vv. 29–30n.
For you are just in all you have done; all your works are true and your ways right, and all your judgments are true.
4 : The prayer emphasizes that God is in all God's actions (see also Dan 9.7).
You have executed true judgments in all you have brought upon us and upon Jerusalem, the holy city of our ancestors; by a true judgment you have brought all this upon us because of our sins.
5 : , v. 8, Neh 9.33, and Tob 3.2–5 all understand the Babylonian exile as the result of the community's sins.
For we have sinned and broken your law in turning away from you; in all matters we have sinned grievously.
We have not obeyed your commandments, we have not kept them or done what you have commanded us for our own good.
So all that you have brought upon us, and all that you have done to us, you have done by a true judgment.

6–8 : : although the three Jewish courtiers are blameless in Dan 3, the song begins with a confession that the community has broken the Sinai covenant and is deserving of God's judgment (see also Dan 9.5). The communal confession, which does not really fit the context of Dan 3 or the court tales as a whole, is similar to Dan 9.5,15; Ezra 9.6; Neh 9.26.
You have handed us over to our enemies, lawless and hateful rebels, and to an unjust king, the most wicked in all the world.

9 : would typically refer to Nebuchadnezzar, the Babylonian king who destroyed Jerusalem in 587 (see Dan 1.1n.). However, the book of Daniel usually depicts Nebuchadnezzar as a king who eventually confesses the God of Israel. The phrase can also refer to Antiochus IV (1 Macc 1.10; Dan 7.8n.; 8.9n.; 11.21–28n.).
And now we cannot open our mouths; we, your servants who worship you, have become a shame and a reproach.
For your name's sake do not give us up forever, and do not annul your covenant.
Do not withdraw your mercy from us, for the sake of Abraham your beloved and for the sake of your servant Isaac and Israel your holy one,
to whom you promised to multiply their descendants like the stars of heaven and like the sand on the shore of the sea.

12–13 : Despite Israel's abrogation of the Sinai covenant, the prayer appeals to God to remember the covenant with and and Jacob (Gen 12.1–3; 15.1–6; 24.14; 28.13–14; Isa 41.8). here refers to Jacob (Gen 36.10–12).
For we, O Lord, have become fewer than any other nation, and are brought low this day in all the world because of our sins.
In our day we have no ruler, or prophet, or leader, no burnt offering, or sacrifice, or oblation, or incense, no place to make an offering before you and to find mercy.

15 : may refer to the Babylonian exile when the king was imprisoned in Babylon, but it also describes the conditions that prevailed in 167–164 after the desecration of the Temple (Dan 8.9–14). A view existed that true prophecy had ended (Zech 13.3–6; Josephus, 1.40).
Yet with a contrite heart and a humble spirit may we be accepted,

as though it were with burnt offerings of rams and bulls, or with tens of thousands of fat lambs; such may our sacrifice be in your sight today, and may we unreservedly follow you, for no shame will come to those who trust in you.
16–17 : , may refer to the self-sacrifice of the three Jews. , evokes Mic 6.6–7 but the sacrifice is an internal reality in place of a burnt offering (Pss 51.17; 141.2).
And now with all our heart we follow you; we fear you and seek your presence.
Do not put us to shame, but deal with us in your patience and in your abundant mercy.
Deliver us in accordance with your marvelous works, and bring glory to your name, O Lord.
Let all who do harm to your servants be put to shame; let them be disgraced and deprived of all power, and let their strength be broken.
21 : , see also Sir 36.9.
Let them know that you alone are the Lord God, glorious over the whole world.”
1–22: The prayer of Azariah.
19–22 : A plea for deliverance; see also Dan 9.16–19; Bar 3.1–5.
Now the king's servants who threw them in kept stoking the furnace with naphtha, pitch, tow, and brushwood.
23 : flammable substances used for kindling. Naphtha is probably an oil-based substance (2 Macc 1.20–22,30–36).
And the flames poured out above the furnace forty-nine cubits,
24 : is 73 ft (22 m). The figure is suggested by Dan 3.19 where the furnace is heated seven times greater than usual.
and spread out and burned those Chaldeans who were caught near the furnace.
25 : , so also Dan 3.22. (i.e., Babylonians).
But the angel of the Lord came down into the furnace to be with Azariah and his companions, and drove the fiery flame out of the furnace,

26 : In Dan 3.25 the fourth man appears to be like “a son of the gods,” but here the identification of the man as an is explicit. No name is given, but in Jewish tradition the angel is Gabriel ( 118a-b). Delivering angels frequently appear in other Second Temple writings; see especially Tobit and Susanna. 44–45 (OG).
and made the inside of the furnace as though a moist wind were whistling through it. The fire did not touch them at all and caused them no pain or distress.
23–27: Protection from the flames. A prose interlude that connects the prayer and the song and elaborates on specific details from Dan 3.
27 : , or dew. The verse provides an explanation of how the angel protects the three young men; cf. Dan 3.23–25; 3 Macc 6.6.
Then the three with one voice praised and glorified and blessed God in the furnace:
“Blessed are you, O Lord, God of our ancestors, and to be praised and highly exalted forever;
And blessed is your glorious, holy name, and to be highly praised and highly exalted forever.
Blessed are you in the temple of your holy glory, and to be extolled and highly glorified forever.
31 : , may indicate that the Temple was functioning properly in a state of purity (cf. Ps 29.9) or may refer to the heavenly temple (Ps 11.4; Hab 2.20).
Blessed are you who look into the depths from your throne on the cherubim, and to be praised and highly exalted forever.
Blessed are you on the throne of your kingdom, and to be extolled and highly exalted forever.
Blessed are you in the firmament of heaven, and to be sung and glorified forever.
28–34: The song of exaltation. These verses address God as ruler of the universe (cf. Pss 96; 97).

32–34 : , winged angelic creatures who form the divine on which God is seated in and in the inner sanctuary of the Jerusalem Temple (Ex 25.18–20; 2 Sam 6.2; 1 Kings 6.23–28; 2 Kings 19.15; Pss 18.10; 99.1; Ezek 1).
“Bless the Lord, all you works of the Lord; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, you heavens; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, you angels of the Lord; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, all you waters above the heavens; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, all you powers of the Lord; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
39 : , the host of heaven (Ps 148.2–3).
Bless the Lord, sun and moon; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, stars of heaven; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
35–41 : Angels and heavenly bodies are commanded to praise God.
“Bless the Lord, all rain and dew; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, all you winds; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, fire and heat; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, winter cold and summer heat; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, dews and falling snow; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, nights and days; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, light and darkness; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, ice and cold; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, frosts and snows; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, lightnings and clouds; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
42–51 : Atmospheric and meteorological elements are commanded to exalt the Lord.
“Let the earth bless the Lord; let it sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, mountains and hills; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, all that grows in the ground; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, seas and rivers; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, you springs; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, you whales and all that swim in the waters; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
57 : or sea monsters such as Leviathan (Ps 104.26; Job 41).
Bless the Lord, all birds of the air; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, all wild animals and cattle; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
52–59 : Geographical elements and nonhuman beings are commanded to exalt God.
“Bless the Lord, all people on earth; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, O Israel; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, you priests of the Lord; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, you servants of the Lord; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, spirits and souls of the righteous; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.
Bless the Lord, you who are holy and humble in heart; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever.

“Bless the Lord, Hananiah, Azariah, and Mishael; sing praise to him and highly exalt him forever. For he has rescued us from Hades and saved us from the power of death, and delivered us from the midst of the burning fiery furnace; from the midst of the fire he has delivered us.

66 : This verse is addressed to the three Jews, urging the youths to also praise the Lord, adapting the traditional praise to the narrative context. , the dwelling place of the dead. , see also 1 Sam 2.6; Pss 16.10; 30.3; 49.15; 86.13; Jon 2.2.
Give thanks to the Lord, for he is good, for his mercy endures forever.
All who worship the Lord, bless the God of gods, sing praise to him and give thanks to him, for his mercy endures forever.”
35–68: A hymn of praise. These verses exhort all living things to praise the Lord (cf. Ps 148).
60–68 : Various groups of people are exhorted to offer praise.
